### Modelling of Rural-Urban Migration: A Statistical Investigation of Western Uttar Pradesh (India)

![](images/portal/abstract.gif)
![](images/portal/references.gif)
![](images/portal/pdf.gif)
![](images/portal/html.gif)

**C. B. Gupta**[1](#Aff1), **Sachin Kumar**[1](#Aff1), **Brijesh Pratap Singh**[2](#Aff2)

**Abstract**

In the present work, we analysed the distribution pattern of male migrants (aged>=15) from western Utter Pradesh through four basic models viz; Geometric, Poisson-Lindley, Negative Binomial and Zero Adjusted Log Series models. Competency of each of the model was tested with the help of F-ratio test and Vyoung test. A bootstrap test was applied to obtain the size and power of the fitted model. Zero Adjusted Log Series model was found to be the best model to explain the migration behaviour of male migrants of this region.

**Keywords:** 
Migration, Maximum likelihood estimator, Bootstrap, Simulation, and power etc

**Cite this paper:** C. B. Gupta, Sachin Kumar, Brijesh Pratap Singh, Modelling of Rural-Urban Migration: A Statistical Investigation of Western Uttar Pradesh (India), *International Journal of Statistics and Applications*, Vol. 6 No. 5, 2016, pp. 293-299. doi: 10.5923/j.statistics.20160605.03.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Utilizing demographic techniques, models like Geometric, Poisson-Lindley, and Negative Binomial aid in quantifying migration patterns and behaviors, providing a statistical foundation for analysis.
Incorporating interviews and case studies allows for a deeper understanding of the motivations and experiences of migrants, complementing quantitative data with personal insights.

enriched_points.point_title: Tools and software used for analysis
enriched_points.expanded_content: Software like R and SPSS are employed for statistical computations, while qualitative analysis software aids in coding and analyzing interview data, enhancing overall research accuracy.
